topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum at a frequency designed to vibrate the fillings in my teeth. Coach Miller stood by the whiteboard, his chalk dust settling like ash on the pristine lines of the game plan. Every word he spoke was a perfectly calibrated wedge driving a spike through my chest. "Look, Jake," he said, his voice dangerously level, "the team needs focus. You're a distraction. Effective immediately, you're off the roster." My hands started to tremble, a fine, uncontrollable shaking that made gripping my water bottle feel like trying to hold water. I couldn't form a coherent sentence. My vision tunneled, the edges of the room blurring into streaks of grey and white. I felt a sudden, intense pressure behind my eyes, a frantic need to bolt, to run until the sound of his voice was swallowed by the roar of the crowd. I just stared at the scuffed toes of my sneakers, unable to process the finality of the decision.

[story 2]

"This can't be happening," I whispered, the sound catching in my throat like shattered glass. My palms were slick, sweating profusely despite the chill in the room. Coach Davies had just delivered the news, his expression a mask of grim disappointment that felt like a physical blow. He didn't even look at me as he finalized the paperwork. I started pacing the small space between the benches, my movements jerky and too fast. My breath hitched, coming in shallow, ragged gasps. I kept trying to organize a response, to argue, but the words dissolved into a thick, hot lump in my mouth. My jaw clenched so tightly that a dull ache radiated up my neck. I looked around the room, seeing only distorted shapes—the reflection in the polished metal of the lockers seemed to warp and swim. A strange, buzzing energy filled my limbs, making my legs feel unsteady, like I was wading through deep water.

[story 3]

The silence after the announcement was deafening, a vacuum sucking all the air out of the room. I stood frozen, my feet rooted to the polished floor, unable to move, unable to breathe properly. Coach Evans had finished speaking, and the sheer weight of his dismissal settled over me, crushing the air from my lungs. My chest felt tight, constricted, as if someone had wrapped steel bands around my ribs. I could feel a strange, hot flush spreading across my face, and my skin prickled with an almost unbearable tension. My eyes darted around, seeking an escape route that didn't exist. A high-pitched ringing started in my ears, drowning out everything else. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the swirling chaos inside my head into stillness, but the internal noise was overwhelming, a relentless, frantic drumming that made my whole body vibrate with uncontrolled energy.
